question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to address the rise of issues connected with Houses in Multiple Occupation.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any engagement his Department has had with local councils, and in particular Belfast City Council, regarding rising concerns attached to Houses in Multiple Occupation.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the area of Belfast that has the largest number of Houses in Multiple Occupation.
To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Houses in Multiple Occupation currently in (i) Belfast; and (ii) the South Belfast and Mid Down constituency.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any plans his Department has in place to assist women suffering from endometriosis.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail the timeline for reducing the current average waiting time of ten years for an endometriosis diagnosis.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department has taken in relation to damage to the roof and subsequent closure of the assembly hall at Rosetta Primary School, given that this issue has persisted for many years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for minor works at Rosetta Primary School. The EA appointed an external architect-led design team in January 2025 to review the works required to the roof of the school’s assembly hall. Following an initial structural survey, the scope of the proposed works was extended, and a more extensive condition survey was initiated. The EA received the formal condition report from the design team on 4 March 2026, and this will now be incorporated into a feasibility report, to inform the business case. It is anticipated that the feasibility report will be completed during March 2026. A timeline for the work will be determined when the business case is completed and the preferred option identified. The EA will liaise closely with the school on a timeline for completion of the work and will make every effort to have the work completed as quickly as possible.  While the roof works are being planned and delivered, the EA has established a licence agreement with the neighbouring church to enable the school to use the church hall, with EA covering this cost. The EA has also installed a gate in the boundary wall to enable pupils to access the church grounds directly from the school site.This is yet another example of an ageing building failing to meet the needs of our school community. I remain committed to improving the condition of the schools’ estate and will continue to call for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail a timeline of when Rosetta Primary School should expect urgent repair works on the assembly hall roof to (i) commence; and (ii) be completed.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for minor works at Rosetta Primary School. The EA appointed an external architect-led design team in January 2025 to review the works required to the roof of the school’s assembly hall. Following an initial structural survey, the scope of the proposed works was extended, and a more extensive condition survey was initiated. The EA received the formal condition report from the design team on 4 March 2026, and this will now be incorporated into a feasibility report, to inform the business case. It is anticipated that the feasibility report will be completed during March 2026. A timeline for the work will be determined when the business case is completed and the preferred option identified. The EA will liaise closely with the school on a timeline for completion of the work and will make every effort to have the work completed as quickly as possible.  While the roof works are being planned and delivered, the EA has established a licence agreement with the neighbouring church to enable the school to use the church hall, with EA covering this cost. The EA has also installed a gate in the boundary wall to enable pupils to access the church grounds directly from the school site.This is yet another example of an ageing building failing to meet the needs of our school community. I remain committed to improving the condition of the schools’ estate and will continue to call for urgent, cross-party support to secure the investment needed to ensure that every child in Northern Ireland is educated in a safe, modern and inclusive environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) a timeline of when urgent works to the main hall of Rosetta Primary School in South Belfast will be completed; and (ii) any reason for the delay to these works.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for minor works at Rosetta Primary School. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider changing the operation times of the Carryduff bus lane, from 15.30 to 18.30 to morning hours, given the greater need for morning operation.
answer_text: The Carryduff (Ballynahinch Road) bus lane has operated during morning peak hours, from 7.30 am to 9.30 am Monday to Friday, since its introduction in September 2001. As part of a series of measures which I introduced ahead of the Christmas period, the operating hours of several South Belfast bus routes were extended to include both morning and evening peak periods. This specific bus lane now additionally operates from 3.30 pm to 6.30 pm.There are no current plans to adjust the existing morning schedule, however, its operation will continue to be kept under review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the streets resurfaced in South Belfast, from 2025 to present.
answer_text: Details of the resurfacing schemes are published in my Department’s annual report to Council.My officials will continue to undertake regular inspections and repair defects in accordance with the Department’s Limited Service policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any criteria used for immunology service referrals for children in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) whether they can only be seen if their condition is life threatening.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail any (i) criteria used to grant free bus passes; and (ii) exceptional circumstances attached.
answer_text: (i) A pupil’s eligibility for home to school transport assistance is assessed against two criteria set out in the Department of Education (DE) policy circular 1996/41, namely distance (two miles for primary and three miles for post-primary) and suitable school. Suitable school is defined as a grant-aided school in any of six categories: controlled or other voluntary; maintained; integrated; Irish-medium; denominational grammar; and non-denominational grammar.(ii) The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for determining a pupil's eligibility for transport assistance in line with DE policy. The policy allows the EA to consider whether transport assistance should be provided in cases which fall outside the criteria where there are exceptional circumstances, but it states that such cases should be, by their very nature, exceptional. The EA does not provide a definitive list of criteria which qualify as exceptional as each case is considered on its individual merits. Parents are responsible for providing sufficient evidence to demonstrate that the case is exceptional. The EA’s exceptional circumstances policy is available on its website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there has been an increase in the number of (i) ambulance call-outs; and (ii) Emergency Department admissions, because of e-scooters, in the last 12 months.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of children in school settings with special educational needs, in the South Belfast and Mid Down constituency.
answer_text: There were 4,213 pupils recorded as having special educational needs enrolled in schools located in the Belfast South and Mid Down parliamentary constituency in October 2025.  This figure includes voluntary and private pre-school centres, nursery schools, primary (including nursery units and reception), post primary and special schools. The department receives this information as part of the annual school census return that each school must complete each October.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of children and young people that have had to attend Emergency Department because of e-scooters, since 2025.
answer_text: My Department does not hold centrally collated information on the number of children and young people who have attended emergency departments (EDs) as a result of incidents involving escooters. ED information systems do not routinely record or categorise attendance data to the level of detail required to identify specific modes of transport or device type involved in an injury.However, I can confirm that the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) received 36 e-scooter related calls in 2025 and 2026 (to end of February 2026), with 31 calls conveyed to hospital and five treated at scene.Table 1Time PeriodAmbulance callouts(treated at scene)Calls conveyed to hospital(see and convey)20253272026 (to end Feb)24It should be noted that the data in Table 1 has been provided by NIAS from the C3 Command and Control using the ‘What’s the Problem’ free text field, using the search terms 'Electric Scooter', 'Electronic Scooter', 'E Scooter' or 'E-Scooter'. Whilst NIAS consider this to be a comprehensive search for such calls, there is the potential for some calls to be missed.  It is also important to note that some individuals may travel to hospital following an injury without using the ambulance service, and therefore would not appear in NIAS data.My officials continue to work with clinical and service leads on improving data quality across unscheduled care settings, including opportunities to strengthen injury surveillance where feasible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's capital projects that are both approved and coming forward for South Belfast and Mid Down.
answer_text: There is a significant programme of capital investment currently approved and coming forward across the South Belfast and Mid Down area. This includes a number of road development and maintenance schemes as detailed in the council reports for the Newry, Mourne and Down District Council, Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council, and Belfast City Council, which would cover the South Belfast and Mid Down area. These reports set out the approved projects for both the previous and upcoming financial years and are published on my Department’s website. The relevant reports are available at:Newry Mourne and Down Annual Report to Newry, Mourne and Down District Council 2025/26 | Department for InfrastructureLisburn and Castlereagh Annual Report to Lisburn and Castlereagh City Council 2025 | Department for InfrastructureBelfast City Council Annual Report to Belfast City Council 2025 | Department for InfrastructureIn addition to the Annual Roads Reports, my Department plans to install pontoons upstream and downstream of Shaws Bridge to support improved access to the River Lagan and the canoe slalom facilities at this location.Through my Department’s Urban Drainage Transformation Pilot Project, work is underway in partnership with Belfast City Council and the National Trust to install a number of natural flood management interventions at its Cregagh Glen and Lisnabreeny property. A Sustainable Drainage System scheme is also being developed with Belfast City Council for delivery at Drumglass Park.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any Capital Projects that are approved and coming forward, for South Belfast and Mid Down.
answer_text: Capital Projects located in South Belfast and Mid Down are being progressed under the Major Capital, School Enhancement, Curriculum-Led, Minor Works and SEN Capital Investment programmes, details of projects are listed in the tables below. Major Capital Works ProgrammeThere are four Major Capital Works Programme projects approved to advance in design in South Belfast and Mid Down, two are on-site, one in design and one in early planning stage. Three projects previously announced remain on hold as detailed below.  Major Works projects typically consist of a new-build school or an extension and refurbishment of the existing school accommodation. St Joseph’s College, BelfastOn hold - announced in 2022 and placed on hold due to budget uncertainty.Breda Academy, Belfast On hold – due to an area planning development proposal.I have made funding available to progress the Forge Integrated Primary School and Malone Integrated Primary School through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release these projects to construction tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.School Enhancement ProgrammeThere are five School Enhancement Programme (SEP) projects approved to advance in design in South Belfast and Mid Down, namely:School NameRIBA StageBotanic Primary School, BelfastStage 1 - Technical FeasibilityHoly Rosary Primary School, BelfastStage 4 – Technical DesignSt Bride’s Primary School, BelfastStage 4 – Technical DesignMethodist College, BelfastStage 4 – Technical DesignVictoria College, BelfastStage 1 - Technical FeasibilityI have also made funding available to progress these SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  Likewise, a decision to release to construction tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.Curriculum-Led Capital Investment ProgrammeTwo schools in South Belfast and Mid Down were selected for inclusion in a pilot exercise to benefit from outdoor covered sports space and a new synthetic pitch. Details of the schools and provisions are listed in table below. question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline any policy work taking place in relation to the impact of short-term lets and holiday homes on the housing system.
answer_text: My officials have been working with colleagues in DfE, DfI and NIHE to commission research on the nature, scale and potential impacts of short-term holiday lets on local housing markets. This research is expected to be commissioned shortly. My Department is also currently carrying out research on second homes (or holiday homes) to improve our understanding of any impacts of those on housing markets.Any potential policy work will be considered following the conclusion of both research pieces and after consideration and assessment of relevant findings or evidence.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) the number of prisoners who were released with no accommodation secured, over the last five years; and (ii) any protocol and support arrangements in place, for those leaving prison with no accommodation before they are released.
answer_text: (i) The Prison Service Records Information System (PRISM) is used for the control and management of prisoners and records their latest address. The information in the table below has been extracted from PRISM and details the number of prisoners released in each of the last five calendar years, along with the corresponding number and proportion recorded as having no fixed abode.  YearPrisoners ReleasedNumber of prisoners released recorded as no fixed abodePercentage of prisoners released recoded as no fixed abode20212,56939916%20222,87739614%20233,19147315%20243,17048515%20253,38751815%Source: PRISM – Management Information Note: No fixed abode data is based on each prisoner’s latest recorded address on PRISM (data extracted on 27th January 2026)(ii) It is widely acknowledged that there is a lack of suitable housing in Northern Ireland and this issue is felt acutely by those leaving the criminal justice system. Responsibility for housing sits solely with the Department for Communities and NI Housing Executive; that notwithstanding, the Prison Service have a number of initiatives in place that seek to mitigate against the issue of homelessness for those leaving prison.In December 2023, an Accommodation Management Protocol was introduced to strengthen multiagency coordination in managing housing and related support needs. This protocol represents extensive collaborative working between the NIHE, Department of Justice, and Housing Rights. Further, it provides a framework for co-operation to enable the accommodation and associated support needs of those entering (including those on remand) and leaving custody to be met.Additionally, since April 2024, NIHE Custodial Housing Advisors have been in place in Maghaberry Prison to begin the housing application process prior to release, with plans to extend the pilot to Magilligan Prison and Hydebank Wood Secure College.Shortterm supported accommodation also continues to be available through Approved Premises for high-risk offenders across Northern Ireland. These premises are managed by community and voluntary sector organisations that work closely with PBNI and the Police Service of Northern Ireland.NIPS also works with the Belfast City Council–led Complex Lives multidisciplinary team to enhance support for females most at risk of homelessness on release from prison.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the uptake of the late-night public transport scheme in Belfast city centre.
answer_text: Translink introduced a range of late bus services on Friday and Saturday nights commencing from 28 November 2025. The new services are on the major Metro routes, and four other Ulsterbus key corridors that have no rail alternative: Crumlin, Downpatrick, Dungannon and Newcastle.  The funding required to support the extension of late-night transport services for one year has been on a collaborative basis with funding coming from my Department, other Executive colleagues and Belfast City Council. The early indications are that there has been a significant uptake when compared with last year with Translink recording 30,000 additional journeys with an extra 7,000 being on Metro alone. I have been encouraged by the support that has been given to the late-night transport services. It demonstrates the need and underlines my commitment to ensuring safe and sustainable travel. The benefits are wide-ranging and the boost to the night-time economy is welcome.I hope people continue to make use of public transport including these late-night services throughout 2026 so that together we can continue to build the case for public transport as a good choice for travel.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her Department’s timeline for introducing new hate crime legislation.
answer_text: ANSWERWith your permission, Mr Speaker, I will answer questions 9 and 14 together.  Subject to Executive approval, I intend to introduce the Sentencing Bill to the Assembly very soon. This Bill will include provisions for a new statutory aggravator model for hate crime.The Department has taken an evidence-based approach to inform proposals for protected characteristics in hate crime legislation and I remain committed to delivering the best outcomes for all victims of hate crime in this mandate. Existing protected characteristics (race, religion, disability and sexual orientation) will be retained in the Sentencing Bill.  A power to add further protected groups by secondary legislation (subject to affirmative resolution), should evidence of need arise, will be created. I have also written to Executive Colleagues seeking permission for the inclusion of transgender identity as a protected characteristic for hate crime in the Sentencing Bill.  Based on the statistical data gathered by PSNI and evidence drawn from Judge Marrinan’s public consultation this addition will help increase public safety and afford transgender individuals in our society the same protection in legislation as their counterparts in England, Wales, Scotland and Ireland. Further provisions to support victims of hate crime will be included in the Victims and Witnesses of Crime Bill which is scheduled for introduction in 2026.  Policy development work on the additional protected groups of age, sex/gender and variations in sex characteristics is ongoing and will be informed by findings from the Phase 2 consultation which is currently paused until progression of the Sentencing Bill by the Assembly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether a full equality impact assessment was carried out on his changes to the Social Housing Development Programme, as per his oral statement to the Assembly on 18 November 2025.
answer_text: A full equality impact assessment has not been carried out on the revised Social Housing grant rates. These changes are not a new or revised policy and are entirely in line with the existing methodology for the calculation of benchmark costs and grant that has been in place for several years. Depending on the results of the reviews I announced in relation to how we fund new social housing and the standards we build social housing to, an equality impact assessment may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill will support parents and their families.
answer_text: There will be a number of measures in my Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill which aim to support parents and their families. Flexible working arrangements will become more accessible from the first day of employment, allowing parents to balance work and family responsibilities better. The introduction of carer’s leave will give employees the right to take time off to care for dependants with long-term needs. The creation of neonatal care leave and pay will offer crucial support for parents of babies requiring extended hospital care, providing statutory leave and financial assistance during this challenging period. Enhanced redundancy and dismissal protections will safeguard pregnant employees and those returning from family leave, offering greater job security. Alongside the Bill, improvements to paternity leave will give fathers or partners more flexibility in how and when they take time off.  Collectively, these measures aim to provide greater flexibility, security, and support for working parents, helping them to manage their work and family commitments more easily.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline his assessment of what the Executive requires from the upcoming Autumn Budget 2025.
To ask the Minister of Education whether he will make provision for girls to be allowed to wear trousers to school if they wish.
answer_text: The School Uniforms (Guidelines and Allowances) Bill which I introduced is awaiting Royal Assent.  When it comes into operation the Departmental guidelines for schools about their uniform policy will become statutory and schools must adhere to them.  There is absolutely no barrier in the Bill to prevent a school’s uniform policy from allowing girls to wear trousers.  I support a balanced approach that enables schools to have the autonomy that I think is appropriate in terms of managing their own day-to-day running, whilst ensuring the guidelines on school uniform policies address affordability for parents.  This does not preclude my supporting young people to access their learning in a uniform that meets their needs in terms of comfort and practicality.  If girls want to wear trousers to be comfortable then the consultation process between the school and pupil is the place to address this – not through mandating specific school uniform items in the guidelines.  The evidence I hear confirms that the biggest single issue in terms of uniforms is affordability.  That is why I brought forward this Bill in the first place – to address affordability of school uniforms.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any steps she is taking to address the recommendation from the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland on the need for a nurse-led healthcare model in all custody suites.
answer_text: AQO 2666 22-27 - ANSWER I welcome the publication of the Criminal Justice Inspection Northern Ireland (CJI) report, “Follow-up Review of Inspection Recommendations: The Detention of Persons in Police Custody in Northern Ireland.”  The CJI continues to play a valuable role in promoting accountability, improvement and public confidence across the justice system, and I appreciate the thorough approach taken in this follow-up review.  Strategic Recommendation 3 of the report relates to the implementation of a nurse-led model across all custody suites in Northern Ireland. While this recommendation is primarily for the PSNI to take forward, my Department will continue to support the PSNI wherever possible in progressing this important area of work.  This includes engagement at a senior level with key partners at the Strategic Custody Healthcare Group meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any work her Department is undertaking on the regulation of Airbnb rentals.
To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce legislation to require court approval for all legal cases involving a (i) settlement; or (ii) award of damages to minors.
answer_text: Any compensation settlement for a child that is agreed as part of legal proceedings will be submitted for court approval. Where a settlement is reached without legal proceedings being commenced, there is a voluntary procedure for court approval. If court approval is not sought, the settlement will not be binding under the common law.Following the outcome of a consultation on this issue, I supported, in principle, legislation to require all compensation awards to children to be approved by a court; however, detailed consideration of how such legislation would work identified a number of practical difficulties, in particular, about enforcement of any statutory requirement to obtain court approval. Extensive engagement with stakeholders failed to identify any workable or proportionate solutions so, last year, I wrote to stakeholders to advise that I had decided not to pursue legislation in the current Assembly mandate. However, I hope to commission research to see if there is any useful learning from other jurisdictions and remain open to legislating on this issue in future if a workable solution can be identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline her Department's policy regarding the use of (i) spit; and (ii) bite guards.
answer_text: My Department does not have a policy regarding the use of (i) spit; and (ii) bite guards.The Member may wish to note that the use of spit and bite guards by the PSNI is an operational decision for which they are accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board. Any questions in relation to the PSNI’s policy should be directed to the Chief Constable or the Policing Board.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether there will be specialised training and guidance offered to ensure that the provisions of her Justice Bill, relating to the bail and remand of children, adhere to international human rights standards.
answer_text: It has always been the Department’s intention to issue comprehensive guidance to all relevant criminal justice agencies who will be required to take account of the changes to the legislation on bail and remand for children.  This will be developed prior to commencement of the legislation and will include specific guidance for the police, judiciary and courts, amongst others, to ensure the provisions of the Justice Bill are fully understood and applied appropriately.  Information sessions and/or training will also be offered for those who require it.  The offer of engagement on these issues has already been made to criminal justice partners during their ongoing discussions with officials as the draft provisions in the Bill were developed.  The guidance and training to be developed will provide an opportunity to emphasise the key aim of the new legislation, which is to further enhance our compliance with international human rights standards, in particular the UN Convention on the Rights of the Child (UNCRC).  It will also highlight how the proposed changes will deliver on overarching child-rights principles, such as ensuring the best interests of the child is a primary consideration, and the use of custody as a last resort.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the (i) flexibility; and (ii) operational guidance there will be within the Justice Bill, to ensure that children who reach their 18th birthday while under a youth custody and supervision order, do not have to do so in Hydebank Wood College, given the short length of their sentence left to serve.
answer_text: The amendment to Schedule 2 paragraph (6) of the Criminal Justice (Children) (NI) Order 1998, which can be found in Schedule 4 paragraph (10) of the Justice Bill, was drafted specifically to ensure that there is flexibility in planning for those children who turn 18 whilst in custody.  Rather than a requirement to automatically transfer to Hydebank Wood College upon reaching their 18th birthday, the provisions allow for a decision to be made by Youth Justice Agency (YJA) staff, based on the individual circumstances of the young person.  Decisions will be made on a case-by-case basis and will take account of a range of factors, including the best interests of the individual, their needs and vulnerabilities, their personal views, the length of sentence remaining and whether they are engaged in a specific education or training programme at the Juvenile Justice Centre (JJC).  The best interests of the other children in custody within the JJC at that time will also be a factor.  No person will be held beyond the point where they are 18 years and six months.In introducing this flexible approach in statute, we are legislating for what currently happens in practice.  Staff within the YJA and Hydebank Wood College work closely on this issue and have significant operational experience in ensuring young people are held safely, securely and in the most appropriate location.  No further operational guidance is therefore required in the Justice Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she will introduce legislation to protect children from abuse of trust in all environments and extra-curricular activities.
answer_text: The law on abuse of position of trust contained in the Sexual Offences (Northern Ireland) Order 2008 was last strengthened by way of the Justice (Sexual Offences and Trafficking Victims) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, where it now extends to the areas of sport and religion.  Prior to this legislative change, only young people under the age of 18 within statutory environments such as education, detention or social care, for example, were protected by this legislation.I am aware of the continued need to ensure that the legislative framework is sufficient in protecting our children and young people from those with malign intent. The offending behaviours which the abuse of position of trust offences capture are already available for children under 16 in the 2008 Order, but there are important elements to how the abuse of position of trust offences affect 16- and 17-year-olds which require particular consideration and a balanced approach in light of specific Article 8 ECHR requirements which any further extension would fully engage.I am open to changing the law further where this is considered appropriate and consequently, this area is currently under review by my Department.  Should any legislative change be required, Article 29A(4) of the 2008 Order enables the extension of the law by way of secondary legislation which would mean that law change can be affected more quickly without the need to await a primary legislative vehicle.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail when the independent review into the case of Jonathan Creswell will be completed, given its six month timeline from its announcement on 21 February 2025; (ii) whether the final report will be published; and (iii) whether she will present the findings to the Assembly, due to the public interest in this case.
answer_text: My Department has received a draft copy of the Independent Reviewer’s report, which is currently subject to final accuracy checks with agencies. Given the public interest in this case, and as set out in the Terms of Reference (ToR) for the Review, it is anticipated that a summary of findings and recommendations will be published.  The ToR also noted that, in determining the information that may be published and the timing, the Department will be guided by the importance of updating Katie’s family and will also have regard to the integrity of the ongoing coronial process. Notwithstanding, it is my wish to share as much information with the Assembly as possible.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the delivery of the Executive's key priorities since February 2024.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for an update on the removal of asbestos and other toxic materials located on the site of the recent bonfire at Meridi Street in South Belfast; (ii) to detail the timeframe for its removal; (iii) to detail who has direct responsibility for its removal.; and (iv) for an update on the ongoing investigation.
answer_text: The landowner is responsible for the removal of hazardous material from their land at the Meridi Street site in South Belfast.  		Whilst the landowner has begun remediation, and a significant amount of work has taken place on site, they have not been able to meet the deadline of 1 September 2025. NIEA is in communication with the landowners to obtain an updated timeframe.		NIEA commenced a criminal investigation into potential breaches of The Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 which is ongoing. No further comment can be made in relation to this as it may prejudice the investigation.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, in light of the statement made by the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland at the British-Irish Association conference in Oxford, whether any Northern Ireland Office or British Government officials have been in contact with her Department in relation to legacy matters, especially those matters that are devolved and under her Department's remit.
answer_text: I have made clear to the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland a number of times my concerns about the Northern Ireland Troubles (Legacy and Reconciliation) Act 2023, which marked a significant departure from the Stormont House Agreement, blocked access to justice for many people, and caused distress and anxiety for many individuals.  My officials have met or spoken with Northern Ireland Office officials at various stages with a view to understanding the operational implications of any policy proposals being brought forward to address the legacy of the past in Northern Ireland.  They have had no role in shaping UK Government policy, but have been able to offer factual information and views on practical considerations around the implementation of such policy where it touches on devolved responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department was consulted in relation to the British Government's decision to proscribe Palestine Action.
answer_text: The Terrorism Act 2000 (Proscribed Organisations) (Amendment) Order 2025 adds Palestine Action to the list of proscribed organisations in the Terrorism Act 2000.  The Department of Justice was not consulted in relation to this decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has had any engagement regarding the ongoing legal challenges in England concerning the proscription of Palestine Action.
answer_text: My Department has not had any engagement regarding ongoing legal challenges concerning the proscription of Palestine Action, as these are outside my jurisdiction and counter-terrorism policy and legislation are an excepted matter and the responsibility of the Home Office.  I have received a briefing from Home Office officials on the rationale for and implications of the decision to proscribe the group; however, given there are live legal proceedings ongoing it would not be appropriate for me to comment further. The Member may wish to write to the Home Secretary with whom responsibility for this matter rests with any further queries.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered joining the legal proceedings in relation to the proscription of Palestine Action as an interested party, allowing first hand observation of the proceedings with the option of conveying any concerns raised by human rights groups and political parties.
answer_text: Any concerns about proscribed organisations should be raised with the Home Secretary and Home Office as lead Government Department for counter-terrorism policy and legislation which are an excepted matter.   Section 4 of the Terrorism Act 2000 also makes provision for an organisation, or any person affected by the proscription of the organisation, to apply to the Secretary of State for deproscription.  If the Secretary of State refuses that application, the applicant may appeal to the Proscribed Organisations Appeal Commission. Since the policy decision is for the Home Office, the DoJ does not intend to participate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Public Sector Transformation Fund.
To ask the Assembly Commission to outline any plans to facilitate more car parking at Parliament Buildings for staff and visitors.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail a breakdown of groups located in North, South, East and West Belfast which are in receipt of Education Authority funding for (i) youth intervention; (ii) planned intervention; (iii) centre-based work; (iv) detached work and (v) summer intervention work.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s Youth Service administers The Executive Office’s Planned Intervention Programme (PIP). I understand from the EA, that the process of awarding PIP funding to groups who applied to the scheme and were successful through assessment is currently underway.Offers of funding have been made to successful groups and the EA is awaiting acceptance. As the process is ongoing the list of organisations are not yet available. The list of groups awarded funding will be published by 30 June on  https://eanifunding.org.uk/fundedgroups.The EA’s Youth Service does not have funding schemes for youth intervention;centre-based work; detached youth work; or summer intervention work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the City and Growth Deals.

To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on work for a permanent memorial to victims and survivors, as recommended by the Historical Institutional Abuse Inquiry report.
answer_text: We have engaged with the four main victims’ and survivors’ groups, and we are pleased that they are unanimously supportive of plans to place a memorial plaque in Parliament Buildings. We have written to the Speaker, seeking approval to progress this.We acknowledge that some survivors wish to see a memorial that goes beyond the Inquiry recommendation, including localised memorials. Officials have spoken to local authorities and sought input from representative groups and those who are not affiliated with a group. Once potential locations have been confirmed, we will work with the Arts Council to ensure that the views of victims and survivors are taken into consideration when designing a memorial.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme.
answer_text: The completion of the Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme was certified on the 20th of December 2024. All permanent defences are now in place with only minor ancillary works remaining.This scheme, which cost £33million, aims to provide a long-term approach to tidal flood risk management for the city and involved the design and construction of flood defences along approximately 8.5km of the tidal River Lagan extending from Stranmillis Weir to Belfast Harbour.Completion of this critical project will help Belfast manage the flood risk impacts presented by climate change by ultimately delivering enhanced protection for over 3,000 homes and businesses from tidal flooding, including parts of the commercial city centre.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on the appointment of a Chair and Board Members to the Victims and Survivors Service.
answer_text: We recognise the important role of the Victims and Survivors Service in supporting people right across our society who have suffered because of our past. We are grateful for the dedication of the current Board and the VSS staff, and we acknowledge the importance of VSS having a permanent Chair and a full Board complement. We are working to have new members appointed as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on her engagement with the Criminal Bar Association in relation to preventing further withdrawals of service arising from a dispute over legal aid.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 11 - AQO 1476/22-27		ANSWEREngagement with the CBA is ongoing and I remain committed to continuing discussions with a view to ending the withdrawal of services as soon as possible but I can say I am disappointed with how efforts to assuage CBA concerns have been received thus far. We have been engaged since before the withdrawal of services, in writing, by telephone and in person.  Indeed it was very disappointing that, having met me, and having arranged to meet officials again to discuss their concerns in December, the Department learned of the January withdrawal of service in a press release the day before the meeting was due to take place. Despite that disappointment, we continue to strive to address what appears to be an ever shifting list of concerns and asks and we have responded positively.  I delayed publication of my Delivery Plan to consider CBA representations. I committed to implementing a 16% uplift in fees at the nearest opportunity and have been working at pace to deliver that. A consultation to enable an uplift to be implemented in May launched last week. While I cannot ignore evidence which suggests a different approach might  produce better use of resources, I have also provided reassurance that exploration of all policy issues, will, as always, be undertaken in full consultation with stakeholders and I have amended the Delivery Plan to reflect that I will consider some issues in slower time. I also amended the Delivery Plan to make more explicit my intention to explore the merits of an independently Chaired Working Group and Legal Aid Advisory Board. I had hoped the CBA would take cognisance of those efforts and respond positively, as others have, to forthcoming opportunities to engage in detail on policy proposals as the Delivery Plan progresses but I will continue to engage with them  to end this disproportionate action. As I have said previously, it is the victims and witnesses, some of whom who have already suffered immense trauma, who are suffering the consequences of this action. The impact on the system will be recoverable at some point. The impact on victims and witnesses may not, despite our best efforts to help them navigate the impact of the additional delay caused by the CBA action. Officials met with representatives of the Bar Council on Friday and I am due to meet them again tomorrow. I am also due to meet the Solicitors Criminal Bar Association, with whom we have had some very constructive engagement, again tomorrow also.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy when the Social Enterprise Strategic Action Plan will be published.
answer_text: Question 15 – AQO 1227/22-27I met with the Social Enterprise Co-Design Group at the end of September when the initial actions for the Social Enterprise Action Plan were presented to me and discussed. The full action plan has since been finalised and the official launch is imminent. Work has already begun to implement the group’s initial actions to strengthen the commercial base of social enterprises. Champions will be appointed within Invest NI, Intertrade Ireland and local councils, to assist social enterprises navigate the support available already. Improved opportunities arising from commissioning is being explored with my officials and those in Department of Finance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of crimes that were committed by people aged between 10 and 17 in the last 12 months.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of crimes committed.  The recording of information in relation to crimes committed is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI. The Department holds information on cases dealt with at courts in Northern Ireland.  In 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, there were 672 cases prosecuted at courts where the defendant was aged between 10 and 17, resulting in 466 convictions.Note:Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts. Age is at date case was dealt with.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how many allegations of inappropriate behaviour within the Northern Ireland Prison Service have led to disciplinary action over the last five years.

To ask the Minister of Justice, following the findings of the inquest into the death of Daniel McConville, to detail the action she will take to address issues of bullying by prison staff and lack of care for prisoners.
answer_text: There was a delay of some 6 years in respect of the inquest into Mr McConville’s death.  During this time the NI Prison Service and its partners introduced new ways of working focused on a person centred delivery model that aims to better meet the needs of the people committed to our prisons. The NI Prison Service has in place a Professional Code of Conduct. Any allegation of inappropriate behaviour by a member of prison staff is addressed under its associated procedures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any plans his Department has in place to improve employment rights for supply teachers.
answer_text: Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the five main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC, and with limited resources, my officials are working with the employers of teachers to improve employment rights for supply teachers.A review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out Through the TNC and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the impact of the Urban Villages Initiative in South Belfast.
answer_text: Fifty-eight Urban Villages capital projects are now complete, including seven in South Belfast. A further four capital projects are currently being progressed, while two others are being explored. Collectively the seven completed capital projects have contributed extensive improvement to the physical space and as a direct result of these projects, there is increased outdoor space for multi-purpose use across the South UV area, as well as a welcoming shared space for parents and newcomer families to learn new skills and build relationships.A series of community led revenue projects were delivered to improve the lives of people and enhance good relations. UV funding allowed for an annual programme of multi-cultural activities, exposing communities to a diverse range of backgrounds and cultures.		Programmes were established to integrate newcomer families to the area, support was provided to educationally disadvantaged children and children with additional needs, and complimentary treatments were made available to tackle issues around physical and mental health.A number of projects were delivered working with youth across the area to enhance cross community relationships. Projects included digital support, water sports, residential trips and shared youth events. The importance of working with youth was identified to support good relations for the future.A decision on the future of Urban Villages is currently under consideration. This includes several options which may expand on the existing work. We look forward to updating Members once next steps have been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance what engagement she has had with the Treasury to ensure future funding allocations are awarded at or above the level of relative need, as set out in the fiscal framework.
answer_text: The Interim Fiscal Framework is clear, that until budgets are confirmed at the Spending Review, the Executive will continue to plan on the assumption that we will be funded at, or above, the 124% level of relative need for future financial years. I wrote to the new Chief Secretary to the Treasury, Darren Jones, following the general election to lay out my fiscal priorities, including the implementation of the Interim Fiscal Framework, and to highlight the pressures the Executive is facing. I have since met with the CST to re-enforce those messages in person.The Interim Fiscal Framework is delivering. The 124% needs-based adjustment factor for Barnett consequentials, is being applied from the date of restoration of the Executive. This means that an additional £60 million has been provided for the Executive’s funds so far this year and it will increase the Barnett received for the remainder of this year. It is important that the Interim Fiscal Framework continues to deliver, as we work to agree a final framework. It also contains a commitment that the Treasury will consider reviewing the North’s relative need, if multiple, independent and credible sources provide evidence that relative need is different to 124%. My officials are currently working to build the evidence base we need to negotiate for a higher needs-based factor, and I will then press Treasury for that.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for an update on work ongoing across the Department to tackle racism, sectarianism and hate crime in society.
answer_text: Racism, sectarianism and hate crime have devastating impacts on our society, as recent events have shown, and we know that these critical issues require a governmental and societal approach. The current Racial Equality Strategy runs until the end of 2025.  The majority of actions have been completed, with the remaining actions intended to continue throughout the lifetime of the Strategy.The Together: Building a United Community (T:BUC) Strategy, reflects the Executive’s commitment to improving community relations and continuing the journey towards a more united society. Considerable progress has been made, with a number of headline action targets fully achieved.A Cross-Departmental Working Group has been established with PSNI and Housing Executive to address the recent rise in hate crimes. We remain committed to creating a safe and inclusive society.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number applications for succession of tenancy with the Housing Executive in the last five years; (ii) the number of these applications that were (a) approved; and (b) refused, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows.“The Housing Executive does not hold or collate the information in the format requested. “For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Northern Ireland Housing Executive directly. Making direct contact with the Northern Ireland Housing Executive will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the Marriage and Civil Partnership Bill.

To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the Housing First programme; and (ii) what engagements and meetings have taken place (a) with the Health Minister of Health; (b) with the Minister of Justice; and (c) between officials, on the Housing First programme.
answer_text: the requested information from the Chief Executive of the Northern Ireland Housing Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Supporting People programme provides annual grant funding of £264,940 (2023/24) to DePaul to deliver 44 units of support, across Belfast and Derry/Londonderry, through the Housing First model. The Housing Executive sought and received additional funding in 2023/24 and £11,336 was also allocated to this service on a non-recurrent basis in March 2024.In order to shape any expansion of Housing First the Housing Executive has commissioned independent research, guided by a Research Advisory Group, that includes a range of statutory and voluntary partners, including the Department for Communities.”  My officials receive quarterly updates on the Housing First programme at the Homelessness Strategy Steering Group at which the Departments of Health and Justice are represented.  However, I am yet to directly engage with Ministerial colleagues in Health and Justice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) whether he will protect the Neighbourhood Renewal budget for 2024-25; and (ii) on what date groups will be informed of the decision on their funding beyond June 2024.
answer_text: I have made the decision to protect the level of Resource funding provided to Neighbourhood Renewal groups, despite the significant shortfall in both the Resource and Capital budget allocations for my Department.  I have also made £1.45M of additional Resource funding available in 2024-25 for organisations  in receipt of funding support from Community Empowerment Division (CED) and Voluntary & Community Division (VCD), this includes Neighbourhood Renewal groups.Officials are in touch with groups as they work to ensure that all grant funding agreements, for the 2024/25 financial year, are in place by 30 June 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities when he plans to hold the next meeting of the Strategic Partnership Group for the lower Ormeau and wider university area.
answer_text: The Strategic Partnership Group will meet again if it is required to consider any strategic issues escalated from the Wider University and Lower Ormeau Inter-Agency group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to outline the impact of social value in the public procurement process.
answer_text: Social value in public contracts can make a real difference to the lives of some of the most disadvantaged people in society. Embedding social value in public procurement provides a significant opportunity to meaningfully contribute to the economic, environmental and social outcomes here. To date positive outcomes have included:•	3,500+ people who were long-term unemployed or disadvantaged in the labour market have gained employment•	75+ social enterprises are now part of government supply chains•	2,700+ hours of volunteering with the Voluntary, Community and Social Enterprises sector•	6,500+ hours of skills development activities •	2,800+ weeks of work experience •	Over 3,500 hours of environmental awareness initiatives •	2400+ hours of health and well-being initiatives

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the flood refurbishment work on the River Lagan.
answer_text: My Department is currently undertaking refurbishment works to water level control structures along the River Lagan. Works to the Stranmillis Weir were completed in August 2021 and now works at the structures in Hilden and Lambeg are underway and due to be completed in the Summer 2024. It is anticipated these works will cost £1.3 million.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice (i) to detail the members who sit on The Tackling Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime Programme Board; (ii) for an update on the work programme being carried out by the Board; and (iii) for a breakdown of the budget allocation in the last five years.
answer_text: The Executive Programme on Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime has been evolving since its creation in 2016. Following a review in 2020, the Executive agreed to move to a new delivery model for Phase 2 of the Programme which has been in place since 2021. The Programme’s senior governance group, Sponsor Group, is chaired by the Head of the Civil Service and is made up of representatives from the Departments, organisations and agencies that contribute to the agreed set of shared outcomes. The organisations represented are the Executive Office, Department for Communities, Department of Finance, Department of Education, Department of Justice, Department for the Economy, Department of Health, Department for Infrastructure, Northern Ireland Office, Police Service of Northern Ireland, Northern Ireland Housing Executive and SOLACE NI. Others may be invited to attend Sponsor Group meetings on an ad hoc basis with the agreement of the Chair.Sponsor Group members are responsible for providing strategic direction towards the Programme’s goal of creating safer communities, resilient to paramilitarism, criminality and coercive control and considering the Programme’s overall investment profile. The cross-Executive nature of the group helps to ensure that Sponsors from across the Executive and wider public sector focus, and are sighted, on Programme learning.Sponsors are also responsible for championing the Programme in their own business areas and considering and addressing the external factors that will either accelerate or slow progress towards outcomes. Sponsors also each have specific responsibilities to address gaps or blockers to strategic progress, informed by evidence and population level data. In the past year there has been a strong focus through the Sponsor Group on implementing trauma informed approaches across the civil service. Phase 1 of the Programme had a budget allocation of £50 million provided by UK Government and the NI Executive over the period 2016-2021. For Phase 2 the current allocation is detailed in the following table:2021/22 2022/23 2023/24 2024/25 £15.63 million£17.13 million£16.33 million£15.85 millionThere is currently no funding commitment beyond the completion of Phase 2 in March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any plans he has to extend the limitation period for claims under the Defective Premises Order.
answer_text: The Minister for Communities has confirmed that his department is the lead policy department with respect to building safety in Northern Ireland and has accepted the transfer of the 1975 Order to his department by way of a Transfer of Functions Order which my officials are currently progressing.	It will be for DfC officials to explore the way forward in relation to any changes in the limitation periods in Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what consideration has been given to a review of NHS interpreters' pay.
answer_text: Face-to-face spoken language interpreters are independent self-employed contractors who are not employed by the Health and Social Care (HSC) service but are paid for their service through the Business Services Organisation (BSO) at an agreed rate.HSC Interpreters must be registered with BSO and adhere to the HSC Terms of Engagement.  This stipulates an hourly rate of pay as well as a preparation payment.  The Department is aware that pay has not increased for a number of years and the impact this is having on the valuable service that interpreters provide. You will however be aware of the significant financial constraints within the wider Health Service.  There has been a number of correspondences to my office regarding this issue. As a result, I have asked the Strategic Planning and Performance Group to review this matter.I will endeavour to do the best I can to come to a suitable resolution.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how he intends to address lengthy waiting times for adult Autism Spectrum Disorder assessments.
answer_text: The Department is aware of the extensive waiting lists for adult autism assessment and the challenges that this presents to those people who need support. A Review of Adult Autism Services across the region has been undertaken and the recommendations were presented to the Permanent Secretary.  One of the recommendations is the appointment of a Lead for Adult Autism Services - recruitment of this post is currently underway. This role will be responsible for taking forward the recommendations emerging from the review; this will include the management of waiting lists for adult autism assessment waiting lists. Each Health & Social Care Trust (HSCT) is also implementing a number of actions to improve service access and reduce waiting lists:Belfast Trust - continues to recruit and train staff within available resources, however demand for Autism diagnosis and subsequent interventions continues to rise. There is a need for recurrent investment to meet the recurrent demand and also non-recurrent investment to manage pressures that have built up over many years, exacerbated by COVID-19. The Trust also provides an Advice service (BAAS Belfast Autism Advice Service) for individuals aged 16+years and their families/carers which is supported by a multi-agency approach and is delivered in partnership with Cedar Foundation, DEL Careers Advice Service, DES Disability Employment Service, NISSA, NAS, AI, Carers NI and the Belfast Metropolitan College.Northern Trust– The Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD) Service is currently funded for 6.9 whole time equivalent staff to provide diagnostic assessments and interventions to adults in the Northern Trust area.  However, the demand for the service significantly outstrips capacity. Additional funding is required to provide to enhance staffing levels in order to address waiting times for adult autism assessments.Southern Trust – have appointed a new ASD Mental Health Coordinator who is expected to take up post by the end of March 2024. This additional support will enable the ASD Adult Diagnostic service to increase its assessment clinics from once per month to weekly (circa 4 assessments per month from current baseline of 1 per month) commencing from April 2024.  Additional investment and appropriately trained staff will be required to significantly reduce waiting times to Integrated  Elective Access Protocol (IEAP) targets.  South Eastern Trust– have updated their short-term intervention service model to a predominately group-based intervention which then enables capacity to be redirected to completing diagnostic assessments.  They are currently in the process of recruitment and have progressed unfilled posts in a timely manner.  Slippage money from an unfilled post is being used to commission a provider to undertake diagnostic assessments as a way to reduce the diagnostic waiting list. The Trust are also in the process of funding an external provider to deliver a group work programme and as a result the additional capa

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) for her assessment of the public safety implications stemming from the growing prevalence of generative artificial intelligence manipulation of audio and visual content; and (ii) whether her Department has considered introducing measures to help protect against the spread of disinformation or other harmful outcomes, stemming from this activity.
answer_text: (i)  Generative AI has the potential to bring substantial benefits if managed appropriately, accelerating productivity and innovation across many sectors but it will also significantly increase risks to safety and security.It has the potential to lead to a pollution of the public information ecosystem with hyper-realistic bots and synthetic media influencing societal debate. This risk includes creating fake news and personalised disinformation. Within the next few years, synthetic media could comprise a large proportion of online content, and risks eroding public trust. Generative AI tools have already been shown to be capable of persuading humans on political issues and can be used to increase the scale, persuasiveness and frequency of disinformation and misinformation. More generally, generative AI can generate hyper-targeted content with unprecedented scale and sophistication.(ii)  The Department for Science, Innovation and Technology (DSIT) has policy responsibility for online safety and digital regulation. In March 2023, DSIT published an AI regulation white paper, setting out initial proposals to develop a pro-innovation regulatory framework for AI. The proposed framework outlined five cross-sectoral principles for the UK’s existing regulators to interpret and apply within their remits.My officials have been engaging with DSIT on these matters to ensure we can respond to and influence emerging policy development and guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for her assessment of the impact of underfunding public services.
answer_text: While each Minister will be better placed to speak about the challenges faced by their department, there is no doubt that the decisions taken to try to live within the budget set by the Secretary of State have caused considerable damage.Although the additional funding provided by the financial package is welcome it is insufficient to allow this harm to be reversed and it does not provide a sustainable solution going forward. The Executive must be funded at need, not placed on a funding trajectory that will keep it below need for many years.It is also unacceptable to place conditions on the write off of debts, when these are primarily due to the underfunding of public services.
